verdict_reasoning: HMGB1 is canonically a nuclear DNA-binding protein (chromatin architectural factor). However, it has well-documented non-baseline outer-leaflet/extracellular display: actively secreted by activated immune cells (monocytes/macrophages) via non-classical secretion, and passively released during necrosis, pyroptosis, and immunogenic cell death where it appears on the outer face of dying cells before full release. It acts as a prototypical DAMP engaging RAGE/TLR4 on target cells. Anti-HMGB1 antibody programs target the released extracellular pool. The surface-displayed/externalized stage during cell death and activation-induced secretion qualifies as cell-state-induced contextual surface exposure.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Most extracellular HMGB1 is fully released/soluble rather than stably surface-anchored; outer-leaflet display on dying cells is transient and may better fit secreted_only.
